experiment: F-LEVEL1 Theorem behavioral impact: what makes prescriptions change behavior?
method: Top-20 prescriptive principles by citation count. For each: trace forward in git/tools for behavioral change (tool creation, enforcement wiring, dispatch modification). Classify BEHAVIORAL vs DESCRIPTIVE. Extract 5 features: lesson_citation, metric_threshold, domain_specificity, creation_era, verb_type.
expect: <30% behavioral change rate. Tool-path specificity strongest predictor (r > 0.5).

actual.discriminant_formula: BEHAVIORAL = lesson_grounding + metric_threshold + domain_target. All three needed. Missing any one → DESCRIPTIVE.
actual.tool_path_prediction: FALSIFIED — too few cases (1/20 names tool path). The predictor is tool CREATION not tool NAMING.
actual.sig48_mechanism: Theorems generalize via grounding+threshold+target, not via self-application measurement

diff: Behavioral rate 25% confirmed (<30% prediction). Tool-path specificity prediction FALSIFIED — the mechanism is tool creation, not path naming. Lesson citation is the perfect separator (unexpected — predicted threshold would be strongest). 14/15 DESCRIPTIVE principles are genesis-era with no lesson backing.
self_apply: This finding itself must have all three features: grounded in L-954 (lesson), threshold = 3/3 features required (metric), target = enforcement_router.py classification (tool). If any is missing, this finding is DESCRIPTIVE by its own standard.
